skill.name: memory-safety-patterns
skill.description: Implement memory-safe programming with RAII, ownership, smart pointers, and resource management across Rust, C++, and C. Use when writing safe systems code, managing resources, or preventing memory leaks, use-after-free, and buffer overflows.
skill.summary: Cross-language patterns for memory-safe programming including RAII, ownership, smart pointers, and resource management in Rust, C++, and C.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 56 static findings are false positives. The skill contains only documentation and code examples for memory safety patterns. Thread spawning (thread::spawn) was misidentified as process spawning. Markdown backticks for code formatting were misidentified as shell execution. Documentation URLs and debugging tool references are legitimate educational content.

content.value_statement: Write crash-free, secure code using proven memory safety patterns across Rust, C++, and C. Prevent leaks, use-after-free, and buffer overflows with RAII, ownership models, and smart pointers.
actual_capabilities: Implement RAII patterns in C++ with proper constructor/destructor resource management
Apply Rust ownership and borrowing rules to prevent memory errors at compile time
Use smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr) for automatic memory management
Implement safe resource management in C using goto-cleanup and opaque pointer patterns
Apply bounds checking techniques to prevent buffer overflows in C++ and Rust
Implement thread-safe data structures using mutexes, atomics, and thread-safe containers
limitations: Does not provide runtime memory safety verification tools
Does not include memory profiling or leak detection implementations
Does not cover garbage-collected languages like Go or Java
Cannot retroactively make unsafe legacy code memory-safe

use_cases.title: Systems Programming
use_cases.description: Write operating systems, drivers, or embedded software that is free from memory corruption vulnerabilities.
use_cases.target_user: Systems programmers building high-performance, secure software

use_cases.title: Security-Critical Applications
use_cases.description: Build applications where memory safety is mandatory: cryptography, network services, file processing.

use_cases.title: Legacy Code Modernization
use_cases.description: Apply memory safety patterns when refactoring C/C++ codebases to reduce bugs and improve reliability.

prompt_templates.title: Basic RAII Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to implement a RAII pattern in C++ for a file handle class. Include proper copy/move semantics and ensure the file is automatically closed when the object goes out of scope.

prompt_templates.title: Rust Ownership Example
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Rust ownership prevents use-after-free bugs. Show an example with a struct that holds a reference, including lifetime annotations and when to use Box, Rc, and Arc.

prompt_templates.title: C Resource Management
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a C function that opens a file, allocates memory, and processes data. Use the goto-cleanup pattern to ensure all resources are properly freed on error.

prompt_templates.title: Thread-Safe Counter
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a thread-safe counter in both C++ using atomics and mutex, and Rust using Arc and Mutex. Show how each language prevents data races.

output_examples.input: How do I prevent memory leaks when opening files in C++?
output_examples.output: Use RAII with a smart file wrapper class or std::fstream. The file automatically closes when the object is destroyed, even if an exception occurs. Example: std::fstream file("data.txt"); - destructor handles cleanup automatically.

output_examples.input: What is the difference between Box, Rc, and Arc in Rust?
output_examples.output: Box<T> for single ownership with heap allocation. Rc<T> for shared ownership (single-threaded) - increments reference count on clone. Arc<T> for atomic shared ownership across threads - thread-safe reference counting.

output_examples.input: How do I handle bounds checking safely?
output_examples.output: In C++, use std::vector::at() which throws std::out_of_range, or std::span for array views. In Rust, indexing with [] panics on out-of-bounds, while .get() returns Option for safe handling. Prefer iterators to avoid manual bounds checking.

best_practices: Prefer RAII and smart pointers over manual memory management to ensure automatic cleanup
Use the borrow checker in Rust rather than fighting it - it prevents real bugs
Run AddressSanitizer and Valgrind during development to catch memory issues early
anti_patterns: Using raw pointers in C++ instead of smart pointers - leads to leaks and dangling pointers
Ignoring compiler warnings about dangling references - they indicate real bugs
Using unsafe blocks in Rust without documenting why and isolating them from safe code

faq.question: What is RAII and why does it matter?
faq.answer: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) ties resource lifetime to object lifetime. When the object goes out of scope, its destructor runs and releases resources. This prevents leaks and ensures cleanup even when exceptions occur.

faq.question: How does Rust prevent memory bugs?
faq.answer: Rust uses an ownership system where each value has exactly one owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped. The borrow checker enforces these rules at compile time, preventing use-after-free, double-free, and data races.

faq.question: When should I use smart pointers in C++?
faq.answer: Use unique_ptr for single ownership (default choice). Use shared_ptr when multiple parts of code need to own the same object. Use weak_ptr to break cycles in shared_ptr graphs. Avoid raw pointers unless interfacing with C code.

faq.question: What is the goto-cleanup pattern in C?
faq.answer: A pattern where all resource allocations happen at the start, and a single cleanup label frees everything in reverse order. This ensures resources are freed even when errors occur at any point in the function, avoiding leaks.

faq.question: How do I choose between Rust and C++ for memory safety?
faq.answer: Rust provides stronger compile-time guarantees through ownership and borrowing. C++ requires more discipline but gives more control. Choose Rust for new projects where memory safety is critical. Use C++ when interfacing with existing C++ codebases or when maximum performance is needed.

faq.question: What debugging tools help catch memory issues?
faq.answer: AddressSanitizer (ASan) catches use-after-free, leaks, and buffer overflows. Valgrind detects memory errors in C/C++. Rust Miri detects undefined behavior. ThreadSanitizer finds data races. Use these tools during development and in CI pipelines.
